skill.name: infsh-cli
skill.description: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover, run, and manage cloud-hosted AI apps for images, video, text, search, 3D, and automation.
skill.summary: Run inference.sh AI apps from an agent workflow using the belt CLI.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, credential, and filesystem patterns. Review found these are mostly markdown examples for installing and using the inference.sh belt CLI, with no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent. Publication should include a warning because the skill encourages remote installer execution, authenticated cloud API use, and automatic upload of local files.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends `curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh` for installing or reinstalling the CLI. This executes a network-delivered shell script and requires trust in the remote endpoint, although the skill also documents a manual checksum-based install path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit and repeated. Context shows a legitimate installer for the documented CLI, so this is a real operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Cloud CLI Can Upload Local Files
medium_findings.description: The skill explains that local file paths are automatically uploaded when used as app inputs. Agents could expose sensitive local files if prompts or workflows provide broad paths without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly states that local paths are uploaded and gives absolute, relative, and home-directory examples. This is expected product behavior but creates a clear data exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: API Key And Local Credential Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents browser login, local credential storage, and use of `INFSH_API_KEY` for scripts. This is normal for a CLI skill, but marketplace users need to understand that authenticated runs can spend quota and access account resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key and credential references are explicit, but there is no evidence that the markdown exfiltrates secrets. The risk depends on how agents invoke the authenticated CLI.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified As Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Many static findings identify inline command examples and fenced bash snippets as Ruby or shell execution. These are documentation examples for `belt app list`, `belt app run`, and related CLI commands, not executable code embedded in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are markdown reference pages and the flagged backticks are formatting for commands. I found no Ruby source file or hidden command runner in these locations.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Found In Reviewed Files
low_findings.description: Review looked for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and requests to skip analysis. No evidence found in SKILL.md or the referenced markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no suspicious instruction-override phrases. The listed location only identifies the reviewed skill header, not a detected injection string.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote install commands are convenient but execute downloaded shell content. Users should prefer the documented manual install and checksum verification path when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and manual alternative are visible in the documentation. The risk is inherent to remote shell installers, not inferred from hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Local File Uploads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI uploads local file inputs to cloud apps. This can expose private media, documents, or directory-relative files if the agent is given sensitive paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is directly described and examples include absolute, relative, and home-directory paths. Legitimate use is possible, so this remains a warning rather than a block.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Network Service Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: Most commands call hosted apps through inference.sh and may return cloud-hosted output URLs. Users should review prompts, inputs, and account permissions before running agent-driven tasks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples consistently invoke remote AI apps and document URL outputs. This is core functionality, but it expands the trust boundary beyond the local workspace.

content.user_title: Run inference.sh AI apps from your agent
content.value_statement: Teams need a fast way to call many hosted AI models without building separate integrations. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through the inference.sh belt CLI for discovery, execution, and task tracking.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and authenticate the inference.sh belt CLI.
Lists and searches available inference.sh apps by category, query, featured status, and pagination.
Runs hosted apps with inline JSON input or saved input files.
Generates sample inputs, checks task status, and saves task results.
Documents local file inputs for image, video, audio, media, and social automation workflows.
Provides reference commands for app development, deployment, completions, and related skills.
limitations: Requires the external belt CLI and an inference.sh account for authenticated commands.
May upload local files to cloud services when file paths are used as inputs.
Does not include the source code of the remote installer or hosted apps.
Model availability, pricing, quotas, and app behavior depend on inference.sh services.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI media workflows
use_cases.description: Generate images, videos, audio, and 3D assets from the command line while testing model options.

use_cases.title: Automate model execution
use_cases.description: Run hosted AI apps from scripts, track long-running tasks, and save outputs for pipeline steps.

use_cases.title: Explore hosted app catalogs
use_cases.description: Search available inference.sh apps, inspect schemas, and compare categories before choosing a service.

prompt_templates.title: Find an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the infsh-cli skill to find inference.sh apps for image generation. Show the belt commands I should run and explain what each command returns.

prompt_templates.title: Run a simple model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use infsh-cli to run a hosted text or image app with inline input. Choose a safe example and include the command plus expected result type.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a repeatable workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use infsh-cli to create a workflow that generates a sample input file, edits it, runs an app, checks task status, and saves the result.

prompt_templates.title: Review a file-upload workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use infsh-cli references to plan a local media upload workflow. Identify which files would be uploaded and ask for confirmation before any command runs.

output_examples.input: Find video generation apps for a short product demo.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists relevant inference.sh video apps, suggests search commands, and recommends checking each app schema before running a job.

output_examples.input: Run an image prompt and save the output.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a belt app command, explains the required input fields, and describes that the result may be a cloud URL for the generated image.

output_examples.input: Use a local audio file for an avatar video.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the exact local file path that would be uploaded, asks for approval, and then outlines the belt command and task tracking step.

best_practices: Review any command that installs software, authenticates, spends credits, or uploads files before running it.
Use sample input files and app schema checks before submitting expensive or long-running jobs.
Keep API keys in approved secret storage and avoid pasting them into prompts or shared logs.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell in sensitive environments without independent verification.
Do not pass broad paths, home directories, or confidential files as app inputs without explicit approval.
Do not let an agent post to social platforms or consume account quota without a human review step.

faq.question: What does this skill help an agent do?
faq.answer: It helps an agent use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover apps, run hosted AI models, and track results.

faq.question: Does it require an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. Authenticated commands require login or an API key configured for the belt CLI.

faq.question: Can it upload local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI can upload local file paths when an app input accepts a URL or file reference.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings. The reviewed files show CLI documentation, not confirmed malicious code.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses remote services, authentication, local file uploads, and a pipe-to-shell install command.

faq.question: What should users verify first?
faq.answer: Users should verify the CLI installer, confirm account permissions, and approve any file path before upload.
